21: vealu moshim behar tziyon lishpot et har Esav vehaitah lADONAI hamelukah: 'Saviors shall go up to Mount Zion to rule Mount Esau, and the kingdom shall be the LORD's.' Obadiah's final verse is the OT's shortest prophetic book's most expansive declaration: the kingdom belongs to YHWH. Edom (the persistent enemy of Israel, descended from Esau) will be judged; the deliverers (moshim, saviors/deliverers) will ascend Zion; the kingdom is YHWH's alone. The NT takes the moshim (saviors/deliverers) as the saints who reign with Christ (Rev 20:4-6) and the kingdom as Christ's eternal reign (Rev 11:15).
1: chazon ovadyah: “the vision of Obadiah” — chazon (vision, from chazah, to see) is the standard prophetic superscription for oracular vision (cf. Isa 1:1; Nah 1:1); it distinguishes visionary reception from the more general davar (word). The name ovadyah means “servant/worshipper of YHWH” (eved YHWH); the brevity of the superscription (no king, no date, no genealogy) is unusual. koh amar adonai YHWH le'edom: “thus says the Lord GOD concerning Edom” — the koh amar (messenger formula) marks the oracle as direct divine speech. shema'nu shemu'ah me'et YHWH vetzir bagoyim shulach qumu venaqumah aleha lamilchamah: “we have heard a report from the LORD and a messenger has been sent among the nations: Rise up! Let us rise against her for battle!” — a narrative report of the divine council dispatching a herald (tzir) to summon the nations against Edom; cf. Jer 49:14 which nearly duplicates v1-4.
2: hinei qaton natatikha bagoyim: “Behold, I have made you small among the nations” — qaton (small, insignificant) reverses Edom's self-assessment of invulnerability (v3); the prophetic perfect (natatikha, I have made you) treats the coming judgment as already accomplished from the divine vantage point. bazuy attah me'od: “you are utterly despised” — bazuy (despised, passive participle of bazah) — the contempt language reverses the honor that Edom claimed; the depth of the reversal (utterly, me'od) signals that no part of Edom's status will survive the judgment.
3: zadon libbeka hissi'akha: “the pride of your heart has deceived you” — zadon (arrogance, insolence, from zud, to boil/seethe) — self-asserting pride that rebels against legitimate authority; hissi'akha (has deceived/misled you, hiphil of nasha') — pride is personified as the deceiver, a motif common in the wisdom tradition (Prov 16:18). yoshev bechagvei sela marom shivto omer belibbo mi yoridenni aretz: “you who dwell in the clefts of the rock, in your lofty dwelling, who say in your heart, Who will bring me down to earth?” — sela (rock, cliff) is both the generic term for cliff and the name of Petra/Sela, Edom's rose-red sandstone capital carved into the cliffs of Wadi Musa; the wordplay is precise. The rhetorical challenge “who will bring me down?” presumes impregnable geography as permanent security — the theological error the oracle dismantles.
4: im tagbiah kannesher: “though you soar high like the eagle” — nesher (eagle/griffon vulture) is the highest-flying bird in the Levantine sky, a symbol of imperial power (Deut 28:49; Hab 1:8); Edom's mountain heights made the eagle-comparison self-evident for pride. uvein kochavim sim qinekha: “and though you set your nest among the stars” — kochavim (stars) echoes the Babylonian mythological ambition of ascending to heaven (Isa 14:13-14: “I will ascend to heaven; I will set my throne above the stars of God”); mountain heights become hubris scaled to cosmic dimensions. misham oridekha ne'um YHWH: “from there I will bring you down, declares the LORD” — YHWH answers v3's rhetorical challenge directly; ne'um YHWH (oracle/utterance of the LORD) asserts divine authority.
5: im gannabim ba'u lekha: “if thieves came to you” — gannabim (thieves) opens a qal vahomer (light-to-heavy) argument: even thieves, who steal selectively, leave something behind; even grape-gatherers (olelot, gleanings required by law to be left for the poor, Lev 19:9-10) leave remnants. ey nidmeta: “how you have been destroyed!” — a sudden interjection of prophetic horror at the totality of the coming destruction; the exclamation breaks the conditional argument. halo yashiru olelot: “would they not leave gleanings?” — the rhetorical point: even minimal human robbers show more restraint than the total divine judgment on Edom. The scale of YHWH's judgment exceeds the most thorough human plunderers.
6: eich nefpessu Esav: “how Esau has been ransacked!” — nifssah (ransacked, searched out, niphal of chapas) — Esau is the patriarch-name used for the entire Edomite people throughout the oracle. niveu matpunav: “his hidden treasures have been sought out” — matpunav (hidden treasures, from taman, to hide/bury) — the buried storehouses and concealed caches that Petra's rock-carved chambers could protect from human thieves; the geography that guarded Edom's wealth from men (v5) offers no protection from divine judgment. The contrast with v5 is intentional: what thieves would miss, YHWH finds.
7: anshei veriteka: “the men of your covenant” — beriteka (your covenant, your treaty) — political alliances described in covenant-treaty language, reflecting the formal diplomatic relationships of the ancient Near East; Edom's position on major trade routes (the King's Highway) gave it extensive treaty relationships. anshei shelemekha: “the men of your peace” — parallel idiom for treaty partners; shelemekha (those at peace with you) = allies. lechem simu mazor lach: “those who ate your bread set a trap under you” — lechem (bread) here means the covenant meals between treaty partners (cf. Ps 41:9: “he who ate my bread has lifted his heel against me”); covenant meals enacted the bond, making betrayal through shared meals uniquely treacherous. ein tevunah bo: “there is no discernment in him” — Edom's famous wisdom (v8-9) has failed to detect the betrayal in its own alliances.
8: va'avadeti chachamim me'Edom: “I will destroy the wise from Edom” — chachamim (wise men, sages) — Edom was celebrated in the ancient Near East for its wisdom tradition; the “wise men of Teman” were proverbial (Jer 49:7: “Is there no longer wisdom in Teman?”; Job's friend Eliphaz is from Teman, Job 2:11). YHWH's judgment destroys not just military strength but the intellectual and governing capacity. uvina mehar Esav: “and understanding from Mount Esau” — binah (understanding, discernment) paired with chochmah (wisdom) is the standard wisdom-literature doublet (Prov 4:5, 7); stripping both leaves Edom without the capacity to govern or survive. halo bayom hahu ne'um YHWH: “will I not on that day, declares the LORD” — bayom hahu (on that day) links the specific judgment against Edom to the broader eschatological Day of the LORD (v15).
9: vechattu giborekha Teman: “your mighty men shall be dismayed, O Teman” — chattu (dismayed, broken in courage, from chatat) — military terror that renders warriors incapable of fighting; Teman (literally “south”) was the northern district of Edom (Jer 49:20; Ezek 25:13), named parallel to “Mount Esau” as a synecdoche for the whole country. lema'an yikaret ish mehar Esav miqqatel: “so that every man from Mount Esau will be cut off by slaughter” — yikaret (be cut off) is the karet penalty formula from covenant law applied to an entire nation; miqqatel (by killing/slaughter) emphasizes violent elimination, not merely exile or absence.
10: mechamas achikha ya'akov: “because of the violence done to your brother Jacob” — chamas (violence, wrong, injustice) is the comprehensive term for violent wrongdoing against those to whom one has obligations; crucially achikha (your brother) names the relationship that makes the chamas uniquely heinous: Edom and Israel are brothers through Isaac (Gen 25:23-26; 36:1). Fraternal betrayal is the theological center of the entire indictment. vekhistekha bushah: “shame shall cover you” — bushah (shame, disgrace) — in the honor-shame culture of the ancient Near East, shame was public and communal, not merely internal. venichrat le'olam: “and you shall be cut off forever” — le'olam (forever) intensifies the karet threat of v9; not temporary defeat but permanent erasure from history.
11: beyom 'om'dekha minneged: “on the day you stood aloof” — the first of the sevenfold yom (day) sequence in v11-14, each specifying a moment in Jerusalem's fall when Edom failed and then actively participated; 'om'dekha minneged (standing aloof, standing at a distance) — passive non-intervention during crisis violates the brother-obligation; minneged (from in front of, in sight of) emphasizes the failure was not ignorance but deliberate spectatorship. vegam attah ke'echad mehem: “you also were like one of them” — the moral verdict: Edom's passive spectatorship made it complicit; silence in the face of atrocity against a brother is morally equivalent to participation.
12: ve'al tire bevm achikha beyom nochro: “do not gloat over your brother's day, the day of his misfortune” — the eight al (do not) prohibitions in v12-14 are addressed to Edom as commands, but the deeds have already happened; the commands are an ironic indictment cataloguing what Edom did. Nochro (his alienation, his calamity, from nacher) captures the strangeness and horror of Jerusalem's fall seen from outside. ve'al tismach livnei Yehudah beyom ovdam: “do not rejoice over the people of Judah on the day of their ruin” — ovdam (their ruin/destruction); Ps 137:7 recalls how the Edomites cried “Tear it down!” at Jerusalem's fall. ve'al tagdel pikha beyom tzarah: “do not boast in the day of distress” — tagdel pikha (enlarge/open your mouth) = idiom for boasting and jeering.
13: al tavo beSha'ar ami beyom eydam: “do not enter the gate of my people in the day of their calamity” — sha'ar (gate) — entering the gate was entering the city for plunder; the gate was both physical entrance and civic heart of an ancient city (where law was administered). Eydam (their calamity, disaster). al tire gam attah vera'ato beyom eydo: “do not gloat over his disaster on the day of his calamity” — the repetition of beyom eydam/eydo creates a liturgical rhythm hammering home Edom's precise sins. ve'al tishlahnah vecheilo beyom eydo: “and do not lay hands on his wealth on the day of his calamity” — shalah yad (stretch out the hand) = idiom for seizing; the oracle moves from spectatorship (v11-12) to active looting.
14: ve'al ta'amod al hapereq lehachrit et pelitav: “do not stand at the crossroads to cut off his fugitives” — pereq (parting of ways, crossroads, junction) — Edomites stationed at mountain passes and road junctions to intercept Israelite refugees fleeing the Babylonian destruction (cf. Lam 4:18-19; Ps 137:7); blocking escape routes of the wounded and fleeing was the nadir of the betrayal. Pelitav (his fugitives, escapees). ve'al tasgir seridav beyom tzarah: “and do not hand over his survivors on the day of distress” — tasgir (hand over, surrender, from sagar, to close/shut up) — extraditing refugees to the Babylonians; seridav (his survivors, the remainder) — both terms for the few who escaped; handing them over made Edom a willing instrument of genocide against its own brother nation.
15: ki qarov yom YHWH al kol hagoyim: “for the Day of the LORD is near upon all the nations” — ki qarov yom YHWH is the pivotal eschatological declaration; what began as a specific judgment against Edom expands to universal scope (cf. Joel 1:15; 2:1; Zeph 1:7, 14; Isa 2:12; 13:6); the singular case of Edom becomes the type of all national arrogance against YHWH. ka'asher asita yei'aseh lach gemulkha yashuv beroshekha: “as you have done, it shall be done to you; your deeds shall return on your own head” — the lex talionis principle stated as a universal moral law; gemul (dealing, recompense) is the wisdom term for the moral coherence of the universe — what one does returns to one's own head.
16: ki ka'asher shtatem al har qodshi: “for as you have drunk on my holy mountain” — shtatem (you have drunk, plural) — the feasting and celebrating of the nations (Edom and allies) in Jerusalem after its fall, violating the sacred space; drinking was a covenantal act, so feasting on the holy mountain was profaning the covenant. yishtu kol hagoyim tamid: “all the nations shall drink continually” — the cup of YHWH's wrath, which Jer 25:15-17 expands into a universal judgment tour; tamid (continually, without end) intensifies the reversal. veshatu vela'u vehayah kelo hayu: “they shall drink and swallow and shall be as though they had never been” — kelo hayu (as if they had never existed) is the most extreme annihilation formula in Hebrew, signifying ontological erasure from history.
17: uvhar Tziyon tihyeh peleitah: “but on Mount Zion there shall be those who escape” — peleitah (an escape, a remnant, from palat, to slip away) — the same root as pelitav in v14; Edom cut off Israel's fugitives (pelitav), but YHWH preserves a peleitah on Zion; the pivot is complete. vehayah qodesh: “and it shall be holy” — the sanctuary character of Zion restored; qodesh (holiness) — what the nations desecrated (v16) will be reconsecrated. veyarash beit ya'akov et morashehem: “and the house of Jacob shall possess their own possessions” — yarash (possess, dispossess, inherit) — land-possession language of the Torah; recovering what was taken in the exile; YHWH's original land-grant cannot be permanently alienated.
18: vehayah beit ya'akov esh uveit Yosef lehavah: “the house of Jacob shall be a fire, and the house of Joseph a flame” — esh (fire) and lehavah (flame) together: the northern kingdom (Joseph/Ephraim) and the southern kingdom (Jacob/Judah) reunited as agents of YHWH's judgment; one of the most explicit post-exilic reunion visions — the two kingdoms that divided at Rehoboam (1 Kgs 12) are envisioned as a single consuming force. uveit Esav lekash: “and the house of Esau shall be stubble” — qash (stubble, dry straw after harvest) — the most combustible material, reduced to nothing by the fire/flame of the reunited Israel (cf. Mal 4:1). ve'ein sarid lebeit Esav: “and there shall be no survivor for the house of Esau” — eyn sarid (no survivor) — the same word sarid as v14 reversed: Edom blocked Israel's seridav; now Edom has no sarid.
19: veyarshu hanegev et har Esav: “those of the Negeb shall possess Mount Esau” — hanegev (the south, the Negeb) designates the southern Israelite territory adjacent to Edom; its population will take Edomite highland territory; yarash (possess, dispossess) reiterates the land-inheritance language of v17. V19-20 maps restored borders through a series of territorial dispossessions: the Negeb/Shephelah taking Edomite and Philistine territory; Israelites retaking Ephraimite and Samaritan highlands; Benjamin taking Gilead. vehashefelah et Pelishtim: “and those of the Shephelah shall possess the Philistines” — hashefelah (the lowland, foothills of Judah) — the coastal lowland contested with Philistia throughout the monarchic period; its restoration is part of the full land-grant vision of the remnant's return.
20: vegalut hachayyil hazeh livnei Yisrael asher Kena'anim: “the exiles of this army of the people of Israel shall possess the land of the Canaanites as far as Zarephath” — galut (exile, exiled community, from galah, to uncover, go into exile); hachayyil hazeh (this army/force/company) — the exilic community described in military terms, suggesting a return-army; Zarephath (Sarepta in Greek, modern Sarafand on the Lebanese coast) — Phoenician territory north of Israel, where Elijah stayed with the widow of Zarephath (1 Kgs 17:9-24); the restored territory reaches north to Phoenician coastal cities. vegalut Yerushalayim asher biSefarad: “and the exiles of Jerusalem who are in Sepharad” — Sefarad (Sepharad) is the OT's only occurrence of this name; identified with Sardis in Lydia (Asia Minor) based on Persian-era inscriptions listing a sparda colony; later Jewish tradition applied Sepharad to Spain, giving rise to the term Sephardic Jews.
